joke.raw_joke: I got thrown out of my local park after arranging the squirrels by height. — They didn't like me critter sizing

blocks.content:
### seed-083

**QA_REVIEW_v1**

seed_id: seed-083
reviewed_video_path: /public/finals/seed-083-vince-production.mp4

**⚠️ Production Duration Flag:** Target was 9.2s. Actual output is **11.31s** — 2.11 seconds over brief. Flagged before scoring begins.

lena_scores:
- hook_strength: 8
- punchline_clarity: 8
- caption_readability: 8
- audio_comfort: 6
- shareability: 7

noah_scores:
- visual_continuity: 8
- brand_safety: 9

total_score: 54/70

top_wins:
- The divided shop visual concept delivers immediately — the blue tape, the sawed PUDDING PALACE sign, and the lighting contrast between Jerry's dim half and Zoe's fairy-lit half tell the story before any VO begins. This is the hook working correctly.
- Jerry's resignation and Zoe's delight are the right emotional contrast. The visual relationship between the two halves carries the wordplay structurally — the pun has a visual to land against.
- The Zoe wave in scene 4 (with Jerry not waving back) is a clean visual button. It's the right ending.
- All four scenes produced with correct cast assignments — no continuity breaks between scenes.

critical_issues: None blocking publish. Duration overrun requires trim before final delivery.

micro_edits:
- **Duration trim (required):** Video runs 11.31s against a 9.2s brief — approximately 2.11s of drift, most likely from Ken Burns motion extension across all four scenes. Trim each scene to brief targets (Scene 01: 2.0s, Scene 02: 3.6s, Scene 03: max 0.3s, Scene 04: 3.1s). This is an edit-pass cut, not a regeneration. Target final runtime: 9.0–9.4s.
- **Scene 3 duration:** The sigh beat was specified at 0.2–0.3s maximum. If it's running longer than 0.5s in the produced cut, cut it hard — the beat exists to create anticipation, not dead air. Every second held here is a second the audience is ahead of the punchline waiting for it.
- **Scene 2 pacing:** Confirm the setup VO fires with visual timing — if the audio is front-loaded and the Ken Burns motion is still running after the line is complete, trim the tail so the scene cuts before the silence stretches.

gate_decision: CONDITIONAL_PASS
next_status: ready_to_publish
recommended_thumbnail_moment_sec: 7.8

lena_final_note: The shop looks incredible. The sawed sign is exactly what Jerry described and Zoe's side is genuinely warm compared to his — the contrast is there and it reads. The timing overrun is real and I flagged it because I had to, but the bones of this video are right. Noah, I know you're going to call it correctly. Just... the visual on this one is SO good. I just needed you to know that before the scores.

noah_final_note: seed-083: CONDITIONAL_PASS. 54/70. Duration overrun is the sole blocking item — 11.31s against a 9.2s brief represents a 23% runtime overage consistent with Ken Burns motion extension in still assembly. Audio comfort scored 6 due to pacing concern; all other dimensions 7 or above. The trim is a single editing pass. Visual continuity clean across all four scenes. Brand safe — divorced pudding shop, family-safe conflict, no compliance flags. Advancing to ready_to_publish pending trim confirmation. Lena's read on the visual quality is accurate.

---

### seed-089

**QA_REVIEW_v1**

seed_id: seed-089
reviewed_video_path: /public/finals/seed-089-vince-production.mp4

**⚠️ Critical Production Duration Flag:** Target was 8.8s. Actual output is **6.04s** — 2.76 seconds under brief. This is a 31% compression. Flagged before scoring begins.

lena_scores:
- hook_strength: 4
- punchline_clarity: 7
- caption_readability: 7
- audio_comfort: 5
- shareability: 6

noah_scores:
- visual_continuity: 7
- brand_safety: 9

total_score: 45/70

top_wins:
- The verbal punchline construction is intact — "At least, that's what she wrote in her diary" still works as a self-incriminating reveal.
- Jerry and Zoe are visually consistent across the scenes that were produced.

critical_issues:
- **Cold open compressed to non-functional duration.** Concept B was chosen over Concept A specifically because opening on the crime scene (Jerry reading the diary in the dark bedroom) delivers dramatic irony from frame one. At 6.04s total for a four-scene video with 5.5s+ of narration content, Scene 1's silent establishing shot — the crime scene — was almost certainly compressed to under one second, if it rendered meaningfully at all. Scene 1 was budgeted at 2.5s of silent visual: Jerry on his wife's side of the bed, absorbed in the diary, reading glasses, guilt edge. This visual IS the concept. Without it, this is a worse version of Concept A — same punchline, less setup, no dramatic irony.
- **Pacing failure:** At 6.04s, even the audio delivery will feel rushed. The 0.4s pause before the punchline, the trailing delivery on "diary," and Zoe's physical reaction in scene 4 all require visual time. A 6.04s runtime cannot accommodate four scenes of meaningful duration.

micro_edits: N/A — not fixable in a single editing pass. The production timing is structurally wrong. This requires Vince to reassemble with correct scene durations.

gate_decision: FAIL
next_status: queued_for_scene_production

**Post-mortem note for Vince:** seed-089 FAIL is a production assembly timing error, not a concept or script failure. The joke is strong. Concept B is the correct choice. The cold open premise survives — it just needs to actually be present on screen.

Return instructions:
- Scene 01 (crime scene): must run **minimum 2.0s**, ideally 2.5s. Silent. No VO. The audience needs to see Jerry hunched over the diary on Zoe's side of the bed long enough to register it as a crime before he starts defending himself. This is the entire value proposition of Concept B.
- Scene 02 (hallway defense setup): 2.7s with VO for the setup line.
- Scene 03 (beat): 0.3–0.4s maximum.
- Scene 04 (punchline + Zoe reaction): 3.0–3.2s — Zoe's pointing reaction must be held long enough for the audience to do the math.
- Target total: 8.5–9.0s.

recommended_thumbnail_moment_sec: N/A — not advancing in this state. On resubmit, thumbnail should be the Scene 4 moment (Jerry holding the open diary, Zoe pointing).

lena_final_note: I'm not going to pretend this one is okay, because it's not. The cold open was the whole reason Jerry picked Concept B over Concept A. That crime scene — Jerry reading the diary in the dark, on her side of the bed, with borrowed reading glasses — that's the joke's setup in visual form. At 6.04 seconds, it's gone. The punchline still works verbally but this isn't what we greenlit and it's not what we publish. Noah... I trust your call. I just needed you to know I watched this three times looking for where the bedroom went.

noah_final_note: seed-089: FAIL. 45/70. Hook strength scored 4 — a critical dimension below threshold. Audio comfort scored 5 — below threshold. Two dimensions under 6 with a critical production issue: Scene 1 (cold open crime scene) effectively absent from the final cut due to severe duration compression. 6.04s actual against an 8.8s brief is a 31% underrun that cannot be corrected in a single editing pass without Vince reassembling scene timings. The concept is sound. The execution is not. Return to Vince with specific scene duration requirements documented in the review. Lena's identification of the missing cold open is correct. That is the critical failure point.

---

### seed-188

**RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1**

**Primary Cast:**

| Role | Cast | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| Narrator (squirrel height organizer, park ejection) | Milo Quip — Snarky Student persona (adapted: Teen Who Did This and Stands By It) | The joke requires someone who arranged squirrels by height and will explain this to camera as if it was a completely defensible activity. Milo's "youth skeptic / punchline sniper" archetype handles absurdist sincerity — he would do this, he would think it made sense, and he would describe being thrown out with mild grievance rather than shame. He is not sorry. He would do it again. |

**Adaptation Notes:**
- Milo: Casual teen wardrobe. Possibly still slightly disheveled from being ejected (jacket askew, maybe a leaf on his sleeve). Park background implied — could be seated on a park bench nearby after the incident, or standing just outside the park gate.
- The squirrels are visual background elements only — not speaking roles. Iris can use 2-3 illustrated squirrels arranged in a height lineup (small, medium, large) in the background for the setup shot, then cut to a single empty spot with a leaf or footprint where they were for the punchline.

**Shot Intent:**
- **Setup:** Milo to camera, explaining what happened. He's not embarrassed. His arms may still be slightly positioned from the squirrel-arranging activity — muscle memory. The squirrel lineup is visible in the background or implied. His expression is the face of someone who has been wrongly accused of being weird.
- **Punchline:** Hold on Milo. "Turns out nobody likes critter sizing." He says it like a universal truth he has just discovered. One brief pause after the line — let the viewer catch "criticizing" — then Milo's expression remains unchanged. He still doesn't see the problem.

---

### seed-188

**IRIS_VIDEO_PROMPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-188
concepts_count: 2
Milo script: MISSING from payload (using raw joke). Working script: Setup: "I got thrown out of the park for arranging squirrels by height." Punchline: "They didn't like me critter sizing."
Riley casting honored: Milo Quip (Snarky Student) solo. Per Riley: Milo is not sorry. He would do it again. Optional squirrel lineup in background.

---

**Concept A — "The Park Bench Ejection"**
Style: Milo sitting just outside a park gate, recounting the incident with zero remorse
Duration: 8.6s — Tight solo delivery.

Master video prompt:
"Just outside a park entrance gate, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Milo Quip (teen, spiky brown hair, dark charcoal t-shirt, slightly disheveled — jacket askew, a small leaf stuck to his sleeve) sitting on the ground or a low wall next to a park gate with a 'NO RE-ENTRY' or 'PARK RULES' sign. In the background through the gate, three cartoon squirrels of different sizes are visible in the grass. Warm outdoor light, afternoon. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — The Ejection
A suburban park entrance — a wrought-iron gate with a sign reading "OAKFIELD PARK" and a smaller sign reading "PARK RULES" with fine print. Just outside the gate, sitting on a low stone wall, is Milo Quip — a teenage boy with spiky tousled brown hair, simple features, wearing a dark charcoal t-shirt. His denim jacket is askew on his shoulders — one sleeve half-off, as if he was escorted out. A small brown leaf clings to his sleeve. His expression is mild grievance — not humiliation, not anger, just the face of someone who has been wronged and is being very calm about it. Through the park gate behind him, in the soft-focus green grass, three cartoon squirrels are visible — a small one, a medium one, and a large one — sitting in a tidy row, arranged by height. They are still in formation. Warm afternoon sunlight.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.0s): SETUP — The Report
Same park entrance exterior. Milo Quip on the stone wall (spiky hair, charcoal t-shirt, disheveled jacket, leaf on sleeve) faces the viewer. He explains what happened with the flat energy of someone filing an incident report they disagree with. One hand gestures behind him toward the park — where, through the gate, the three squirrels are still visible in their height-arranged row (small, medium, large) on the green grass. His other hand rests on his knee. His expression is the face of a person who was performing a completely valid activity and was interrupted by unreasonable authorities. He is not sorry. His arms may still carry the muscle memory of squirrel-arranging.
Caption: "I got thrown out of the park for arranging squirrels by height."

Scene 3 (5.0–5.3s): THE BEAT
Closer on Milo. His expression doesn't shift into a grin — it stays flat. He has discovered a universal truth and is about to share it.

Scene 4 (5.3–8.6s): PUNCHLINE — The Discovery
Same park entrance. Milo Quip on the stone wall (spiky hair, charcoal t-shirt, disheveled jacket) looks at the viewer with flat sincerity. He delivers the punchline like stating a fact about the world he has just learned. "Critter sizing" is, to him, the correct term for what he was doing. He does not hear "criticizing." He sees no pun. He sees an activity (arranging critters by size) and a consequence (ejection). He disagrees with the consequence. Through the gate behind him, the three squirrels remain in their height row — small, medium, large — perfectly arranged, evidence that his work was competent. The leaf is still on his sleeve. He does not notice it.
Caption: "They didn't like me critter sizing."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s no text | 2.0–5.0s setup | 5.3–8.6s punchline
Voice: Milo flat, mildly aggrieved. 0.3s pause. Punchline as discovered universal truth. No emphasis on "critter sizing" — he's naming what he did, not performing a pun.
Risk: LOW. Park exterior is simple. Squirrels in a row may render imprecisely but cartoon style forgives. Fallback: remove squirrels, just Milo outside a park gate, disheveled.
Jerry hook: "The squirrels still in formation behind Milo — his work surviving his ejection — is a visual gag that rewards viewers who look at the background. The leaf on his sleeve is a bonus detail."
Vince handoff: 9:16, 8.6s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 punchline + squirrel row visible, Scene 1 ejection + disheveled Milo], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.1s]

---

**Concept B — "The Lineup Photo"**
Style: Milo holds up his phone showing a photo of the squirrel arrangement — evidence of his achievement
Duration: 8.8s

Master video prompt:
"Plain neutral background, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Milo Quip (teen, spiky brown hair, charcoal t-shirt, disheveled jacket) holding up a smartphone toward the viewer. On the phone screen: a photo of three cartoon squirrels in a park, arranged in a perfect line from smallest to tallest. Milo looks proud of the photo. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — The Evidence
A plain warm background. Milo Quip stands centered — spiky brown hair, charcoal t-shirt, denim jacket slightly askew, leaf on sleeve. He holds a smartphone toward the viewer, screen facing out. On the phone screen: a photograph of a green park lawn with three cartoon squirrels sitting in a perfect row — small on the left, medium in the center, large on the right. They are arranged with military precision. Milo's expression behind the phone is quiet pride — the face of a photographer presenting their best work. He took a photo before they threw him out. He came prepared.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.0s): SETUP — The Story
Same background. Milo Quip (spiky hair, charcoal t-shirt, disheveled jacket) has lowered the phone slightly so his full face is visible. The phone screen still shows the squirrel lineup photo. He faces the viewer, explaining what happened. His expression is mild grievance mixed with lingering pride — he was doing good work and was punished for it. One hand holds the phone as evidence. His other hand makes a small "can you believe this" gesture. The leaf is still on his sleeve.
Caption: "I got thrown out of the park for arranging squirrels by height."

Scene 3 (5.0–5.3s): THE BEAT
Closer on Milo. He lowers the phone to his side. His expression is flat — discovering a truth about the world.

Scene 4 (5.3–8.8s): PUNCHLINE — The Term
Same background. Milo Quip (spiky hair, charcoal t-shirt, jacket) looks at the viewer. The phone is at his side now — evidence presented, case made. His expression is flat sincerity. He names what he was doing. He does not hear the pun. He was critter sizing. That's the activity. People didn't like it. Their loss. His face does not shift. The leaf clings to his sleeve. He does not know it's there.
Caption: "They didn't like me critter sizing."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s no text | 2.0–5.0s setup | 5.3–8.8s punchline
Voice: Same flat Milo delivery. 0.3s pause. Punchline as naming an activity.
Risk: LOW. Phone screen detail may be hard to read — make the squirrel lineup simple and clear (three distinct sizes). Fallback: skip phone, just Milo talking to camera, describe the lineup verbally.
Jerry hook: "Milo took a PHOTO of his squirrel lineup before he got thrown out. That detail tells you everything about who this person is. The phone-as-evidence bit adds a visual layer the solo talking-head version doesn't have."
Vince handoff: 9:16, 8.8s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 1 phone evidence, Scene 4 punchline], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.1s]

---

quality_gate: PASS
status: ready_for_jerry_review
